jokes.text: Tony claims having a guy who went to school with Leonard Nimoy as a celebrity fare
jokes.setup: Tony is asked about previous celebrity fares
jokes.punchline: His definition of 'celebrity' is someone who went to school with someone famous
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Tony's innocent optimism and loose definition of celebrity status. The absurdity is earned through character.

jokes.text: What's-his-name.
jokes.setup: Tony builds excitement about his celebrity fare, asks them to guess
jokes.punchline: After the buildup, he can't even remember the celebrity's name
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Tony's excitement deflated by his own inability to remember the name. Good structure and economy.

jokes.text: Latka's impression of Herve Villechaize: 'Hey, boss. Hello, boss. How are you, boss?'
jokes.setup: Latka offers to do an impression
jokes.punchline: His impression is just repeating 'boss' in different ways
jokes.explanation: Classic Latka physical comedy. The repetitive nature and his earnestness make it funnier.

jokes.text: Alex's deadpan response: 'Not bad, but could you do the guy who went to school with Leonard Nimoy?'
jokes.setup: Latka finishes his impression and asks if they like it
jokes.punchline: Alex references Tony's earlier absurd celebrity claim
jokes.explanation: Brilliant callback delivered with perfect deadpan timing. Economy of language and unexpected connection makes this exceptional.

jokes.text: Louie's response: 'Envy is an ugly emotion, Rieger'
jokes.setup: Alex makes a witty comeback about Tony's celebrity encounter
jokes.punchline: Louie misinterprets the joke as genuine envy
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Louie's obliviousness to wit and his tendency toward pompous pronouncements.

jokes.text: Latka's description of The Honeymooners: 'Hey, Ralphie boy... How you doing? Oh, Alice, one of these days, pow! Right, right, right in... right into the moon.'
jokes.setup: Asked about popular TV shows in his country
jokes.punchline: Latka's broken English version of Ralph Kramden's famous threat
jokes.explanation: Latka's mangled delivery of a classic TV line is both endearing and funny. The hesitation and correction add to the charm.

jokes.text: Hey, Ralphie boy... How you doing? Oh, Alice, one of these days, pow! Right, right, right in... right into the moon.
jokes.setup: Latka names a mysterious foreign show 'The Drofnikis'
jokes.punchline: He quotes The Honeymooners with his accent, revealing the 'foreign' show is just dubbed American TV
jokes.explanation: Excellent reveal joke with good structure. The accent making familiar dialogue sound foreign is clever and well-executed.

jokes.text: Louie: 'I don't think you're a dumb boxer.' Tony: 'You don't, Lou?' Louie: 'No. I think you're a bad boxer. I think you're a dumb person.'
jokes.setup: Louie appears to defend Tony against the 'dumb boxer' label
jokes.punchline: He clarifies that Tony's not dumb at boxing, he's bad at boxing AND dumb in general
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and misdirection. Louie's cruel logic is delivered with surgical precision. Classic insult comedy elevated by structure.

jokes.text: Tony recognizes Eric Sevareid and calls the conference 'The Gathering Of Smart Guys'
jokes.setup: Tony encounters the famous journalist
jokes.punchline: His simpleton interpretation of 'G.O.S.G. Conference'
jokes.explanation: Tony's innocent reduction of an intellectual conference to 'smart guys' is perfectly in character and endearing.

jokes.text: Tony: 'It must be tough coming up with opinions all the time. I know... I come up with one myself every now and then. Makes my head hurt.'
jokes.setup: Tony trying to relate to a famous intellectual
jokes.punchline: His honest admission that thinking hurts his head
jokes.explanation: Pure Tony character work. The innocent honesty about intellectual limitations is both funny and endearing.

jokes.text: Tony's solution to world conflict: 'Let the leaders of these countries put on the gloves and go a couple of rounds. The match I'd like to see is Carter-Castro. Castro's got the weight on him. You know, but Jimmy's wiry, and he does a lot of roadwork.'
jokes.setup: Tony suggests leaders should fight instead of their countries
jokes.punchline: He analyzes Carter vs Castro like a boxing match
jokes.explanation: Tony's boxing perspective on geopolitics is both absurd and somehow logical. The specific analysis of Carter and Castro is brilliant character work.

jokes.text: Tony's solution to world hunger: 'Well, like all those fancy White House parties. Right? Instead of inviting all those rich people, invite some poor people. I mean, rich people can afford to feed themselves, right?'
jokes.setup: Sevareid asks about Tony's solution to world hunger
jokes.punchline: Tony's naive but logical suggestion about White House dinner parties
jokes.explanation: Tony's simpleton logic is actually quite sound, making it funny and endearing. The earnestness sells it.

jokes.text: You're no slouch yourself.
jokes.setup: Sevareid compliments Tony's unique perspective
jokes.punchline: Tony returns the compliment to one of America's most famous intellectuals
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's lack of awareness about who he's talking to.

jokes.text: Tony: 'Hey, if I say two smart things, can I bring a date?'
jokes.setup: Sevareid explains Tony just needs to say something intelligent to get into the conference
jokes.punchline: Tony's innocent question about bringing a date
jokes.explanation: Tony's innocent misunderstanding of intellectual elitism. The logic is charmingly flawed.

jokes.text: Tony's confident response to debating Buckley, Kissinger, and Galbraith: 'We'll kick their butt.'
jokes.setup: Tony learns he'll debate famous intellectuals
jokes.punchline: His casual confidence about beating intellectual giants
jokes.explanation: Tony's obliviousness to the intellectual mismatch is perfectly in character. The casual tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Indubitably.
jokes.setup: Tony confidently says they'll kick butt in the debate
jokes.punchline: Sevareid's overly formal agreement contrasts with Tony's crude confidence
jokes.explanation: Great contrast joke with perfect word choice. The formality against Tony's street language works well.

jokes.text: Louie: 'This is tough, Banta. You could be a dumb anything.'
jokes.setup: Louie trying to guess what Tony might fantasize about
jokes.punchline: His conclusion that Tony's stupidity knows no bounds
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie meanness with perfect economy. The phrase 'dumb anything' is brilliantly dismissive.

jokes.text: Latka's fantasy begins with everyone addressing him as 'Mr. Gravas' and calling him 'chipper off the old block'
jokes.setup: Latka begins describing his fantasy
jokes.punchline: He's the boss and mangles the phrase 'chip off the old block'
jokes.explanation: Latka's fantasy of power combined with his linguistic confusion creates perfect character comedy. 'Chipper off the old block' is delightful.

jokes.text: In Latka's fantasy, Louie gets in trouble for going to the bathroom: 'But you went to the bathroom yesterday. Is this going to be a habit with you?'
jokes.setup: Boss Latka discovers Louie went to the bathroom
jokes.punchline: Going to the bathroom once per day is apparently excessive
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor showing Latka's revenge fantasy taken to ridiculous extremes. The bureaucratic tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: In Latka's fantasy, Louie is sentenced to execution by firing squad for not fixing cabs
jokes.setup: Boss Latka punishes employee Louie
jokes.punchline: The punishment escalates to death by firing squad
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation that perfectly captures Latka's sweet revenge fantasy. The extreme punishment for minor infractions is absurdly satisfying.

jokes.text: 'He doesn't smoke' when Louie is offered a last cigarette before execution
jokes.setup: Louie is offered a traditional last cigarette
jokes.punchline: Someone points out the practical issue with this tradition
jokes.explanation: Small but effective detail that adds realism to the absurd situation. The matter-of-fact delivery works.

jokes.text: Latka's fantasy ends with him saying 'Fire' for the execution just as real Louie snaps him back to reality
jokes.setup: Latka's revenge fantasy reaches its climax
jokes.punchline: The timing of 'Fire' coincides with reality interrupting
jokes.explanation: Good timing and structure as fantasy collides with reality. The juxtaposition is well-executed.

jokes.text: Louie's advice about Herve Villechaize: 'Don't give him those back. Charge him for them. You may get a reward... Come on, Banta, he's a big star. He's rolling in dough. He uses $20 bills to train his dog.'
jokes.setup: Tony plans to return photos to the star
jokes.punchline: Louie's increasingly absurd assumptions about celebrity wealth
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie greed with escalating absurdity. The dog training detail is perfectly ridiculous.

jokes.text: Banta, sometimes, you're so stupid I want to cry.
jokes.setup: Tony chose to be nice rather than charge the celebrity
jokes.punchline: Louie's frustration reaches an almost emotional level
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie exasperation with good emotional escalation. The 'want to cry' adds unexpected vulnerability.

jokes.text: Tony's excitement: 'Well, at least I get to do my impression of him in person.'
jokes.setup: Tony is thrilled about meeting Villechaize
jokes.punchline: His main excitement is about doing an impression TO the person
jokes.explanation: Tony's innocent obliviousness to social inappropriateness. His priorities are wonderfully skewed.

jokes.text: Alex's warning about Tony's impression and Elaine asking why he's encouraging him creates confusion
jokes.setup: Alex warns against the impression
jokes.punchline: Elaine thinks he's encouraging when he's actually warning
jokes.explanation: Minor misunderstanding humor. Functional but not particularly strong or memorable.

jokes.text: Bobby Wheeler's fantasy as a famous singer performing 'City Boy'
jokes.setup: Bobby's turn to share his fantasy
jokes.punchline: Extended musical performance fantasy
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate fantasy but goes on too long. The concept works but execution drags.

jokes.text: In Bobby's fantasy, famous Bobby Wheeler returns to the garage but charges Louie a buck to acknowledge knowing him
jokes.setup: Drunk Louie begs star Bobby to acknowledge their past
jokes.punchline: Bobby charges money for the recognition
jokes.explanation: Good reversal showing how fame corrupts. Bobby's petty cruelty toward desperate Louie is effective.

jokes.text: I-I just wanted to come back and see my humble beginnings.
jokes.setup: Famous Bobby returns to the taxi garage
jokes.punchline: He condescendingly refers to his 'humble beginnings'
jokes.explanation: Shows how fame would change Bobby's attitude. The condescension is appropriately obnoxious.

jokes.text: It'll cost you a buck.
jokes.setup: Louie asks famous Bobby to tell everyone he knows him
jokes.punchline: Famous Bobby charges for acknowledgment
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing how fame corrupts. The transactional nature is perfectly cruel.

jokes.text: Louie gives his last buck and Bobby still denies knowing him: 'I never saw the man before in my life.'
jokes.setup: Louie pays the fee expecting recognition
jokes.punchline: Bobby takes the money and still denies knowing him
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation that's both cruel and funny. The betrayal after payment makes it more impactful.

jokes.text: Bob... this is my last buck. How am I going to eat?
jokes.setup: Bobby charges Louie for acknowledgment
jokes.punchline: Louie desperately pays his last dollar just for recognition
jokes.explanation: Effective cringe showing Louie's desperation for recognition. The stakes make it more painful.

jokes.text: Aliens want Jim because 'You don't understand why everybody wants jobs, why everybody wants to get ahead. You don't know what the point is, and you don't care.'
jokes.setup: Jim wonders why aliens want him
jokes.punchline: They appreciate his complete detachment from societal ambitions
jokes.explanation: Perfect character analysis that's both funny and insightful. The aliens understand Jim better than humans do.

jokes.text: Aliens: 'You haven't read a newspaper in years, and you live in fear of someone starting a conversation with you about what's going on in the world.'
jokes.setup: Continued explanation of why they want Jim
jokes.punchline: His deliberate ignorance of current events
jokes.explanation: Continues the insightful character analysis. The specific fear of current events conversations is relatable.

jokes.text: Aliens: 'You're wearing every piece of clothing you own.'
jokes.setup: Continued character assessment
jokes.punchline: Observation about Jim's appearance and lifestyle
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective visual observation. Economy of words for maximum impact about Jim's lifestyle.

jokes.text: Aliens: 'When you talk, everyone looks puzzled.'
jokes.setup: Another character observation
jokes.punchline: Jim's effect on others when he speaks
jokes.explanation: Accurate character observation delivered with perfect economy. Captures Jim's communication style.

jokes.text: Aliens: 'The universe is a source of wonder and amusement to you.'
jokes.setup: Final character assessment
jokes.punchline: Jim's childlike wonder at existence
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character insight that's both funny and touching. Captures Jim's essential nature perfectly.

jokes.text: The universe is a source of wonder and amusement to you.
jokes.setup: Final trait in the alien's catalog
jokes.punchline: Jim's childlike wonder is presented as profound wisdom
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character moment that reframes Jim's naivety as a positive trait. Good emotional payoff.

jokes.text: Jim's casual acceptance: 'Okeydoke.'
jokes.setup: Aliens explain they love and understand him
jokes.punchline: Jim's completely casual response to alien abduction
jokes.explanation: Perfect character response. Jim's complete lack of concern about alien abduction is hilariously in character.

jokes.text: Okeydoke.
jokes.setup: Aliens have explained they love and understand him
jokes.punchline: Jim's casual, unfazed acceptance of alien abduction
jokes.explanation: Perfect character response. Jim's casual acceptance of the extraordinary is perfectly in character and very funny.

jokes.text: Jim to the aliens: 'Wow, you guys are funny looking.'
jokes.setup: Jim is being taken by aliens
jokes.punchline: His only comment is about their appearance
jokes.explanation: Jim's innocent observation shows his complete lack of appropriate fear or awe. Perfectly captures his character.

jokes.text: Wow, you guys are funny looking.
jokes.setup: Jim has just agreed to go with the aliens
jokes.punchline: His first observation is that they look funny, with no self-awareness of irony
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Jim's lack of self-awareness while commenting on others' appearance.

jokes.text: Latka does his Herve Villechaize impression for the real Herve
jokes.setup: Latka performs his impression for the actual person
jokes.punchline: The awkwardness of doing an impression to someone's face
jokes.explanation: Callback to earlier impression creates cringe comedy. The awkwardness is palpable and funny.

jokes.text: Herve says to Louie: 'Uh, will somebody tell the big guy that he stinks?'
jokes.setup: Louie has been obnoxious throughout the visit
jokes.punchline: The very short actor calls Louie 'the big guy' while insulting him
jokes.explanation: Brilliant reversal where the small actor calls Louie 'big guy' while delivering an insult. Perfect economy and unexpected perspective.

jokes.text: Pathetic.
jokes.setup: Bobby is smiling in anticipation of something
jokes.punchline: Louie's disgusted one-word summary
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie dismissal with maximum economy. One word captures his disdain perfectly.
